intro: St Mary the Virgin has served Henbury for many centuries. The building has changed over time, but worship, prayer, and parish life have remained at its heart.

entries.title: Norman origins and medieval growth
entries.summary: The earliest stonework points to a Norman church on this site. By the later medieval period, the church had become a central place for worship and village life.

entries.title: Tower and churchyard expansion
entries.summary: Much of the tower and parts of the nave were rebuilt or strengthened as the parish expanded, leaving the strong Perpendicular character seen today.

entries.title: Victorian restoration
entries.summary: As with many parish churches, substantial restoration work was carried out to repair the fabric and adapt seating and liturgical space.

entries.title: War memory and parish continuity
entries.summary: The church became a place of remembrance for local people lost in conflict. Parish records from this period also show steady everyday ministry through social change.

entries.title: Living parish, active churchyard
entries.summary: St Mary's continues as a worshipping community and a historic landmark for Henbury, with regular services, pastoral ministry, and shared local events.

churchyard: The churchyard includes memorials and graves from different periods, each reflecting the social history of Henbury.
Interpretation of memorials linked to slavery and enslavement is handled with care, factual language, and historical context.
The churchyard also includes the grave of Scipio Africanus, often described as an LGBT landmark grave as well as an important site in Black British history.
